questions.question_text: Whose toe-nail was collected by Mr. Willy Wonka?
questions.explanation_text: Mr. Wonka collected the toe-nail clipping of a 168-year-old Russian farmer called Petrovich Gregorovitch.

questions.question_text: Of what colour was Vita-Wonk?
questions.explanation_text: Mr. Willy Wonka after a lot of labor, finally invented Vita-Wonk. He created a tiny cupful of an oily black liquid in his inventing room.

questions.question_text: What best describes Willy Wonka's attitude when he talks about trees?
Which parts of the animal's body did Mr. Wonka collect?
questions.explanation_text: He collected the toe-nail clippings from a 168-year-old Russian farmer. The tail of a 207-year-old giant rat from Tibet and of a 51-year-old horse in Arabia.

questions.question_text: The oldest living thing in the world is: (The invention of Vita-Wonk)
questions.explanation_text: The oldest living thing in the world is a tree called the Bristlecone pine that grows upon the slopes of Wheeler Peak in Nevada, U.S.A.

questions.question_text: What lives longer than anything else? (The invention of Vita-Wonk)
questions.explanation_text: Charlie suggests that trees live longer than anything else.

questions.question_text: What is the oldest of all options given below? (The invention of Vita-Wonk)
questions.explanation_text: Bristlecone Pine trees live the longest. He took a pint of sap from a 4000-years-old Bristlecone Pine.

questions.question_text: Based on the text, what seems to be the purpose of the Great Glass Elevator?

options.label: A
options.text: It is a vehicle for travel.

options.label: B
options.text: It is a machine to collect special items.

options.label: C
options.text: Ithelps Willy to spot the oldest living things.

options.label: D
options.text: It is the place where Willy conducts his experiments.

questions.correct_answer: It is a vehicle for travel.
questions.explanation_text: It is a vehicle for travel.

questions.question_text: Which of the following animals has not been mentioned by Mr. Wonka?
questions.explanation_text: He tracked down the whistle-pig, the bobolink, the skrock, the pollyfrog, the giant curlicue, the stinging slug, and the venomous squerkle who can spit poison right into your eye from fifty yards away.

questions.question_text: On whom was Vita-Wonk tried and tested?
questions.explanation_text: Mr. Willy Wonka invented a product which he called Vita-Wonk and that was supposed to make people older. It was first given to a brave twenty-year-old Oompa-Loompa volunteer to see its effects before launching it in the market.

questions.question_text: How many drops of the new invention did the volunteer drink? (The invention of Vita-Wonk)
questions.explanation_text: The Oompa-Loompa volunteer swallowed four drops of the new invention made by Mr. Willy Wonka. He was twenty years of age when he swallowed the drops and the aftermath became a seventy-five-year-old.

questions.question_text: How long does the Bristlecone pine tree live? (The Invention of Vita-Wonk)
questions.explanation_text: Bristlecone pine trees live for around 4000 years and they are found in abundance upon the slopes of Wheeler Peak in Nevada, USA.

questions.question_text: Mr. Wonka collects Items from the oldest things to make:
questions.explanation_text: Mr. Wonka decided to invent a new drug that would help people to grow older.

questions.question_text: What was the full name of Mr. Wonka?
questions.explanation_text: Mr. Willy Wonka was a very strange inventor. He used to make all kinds of strange inventions. He had an assistant called Charlie.

questions.question_text: How many of the oldest living things can you remember from Mr. Wonka's list?
What did Mr. Wonka invent?

Mr. Wonka begins by asking himself two questions. What are they?
What is ________?
What lives ________?
What is the special feature of Venomous Squerkle?
How long does this tree live? Where can you find it?
What was unique about the Great Glass Elevator?
Mr. Wonka collected whose toe-nail?
On whom did Mr. Wonka tested the oily black liquid?
Bristlecone Pine trees live the longest. Whom did Mr. Wonka asked Charlie to confirm this fact with?
What does the lesson tell you about Venomous Squerkle?
What enabled Mr. Wonka to go around the world so quickly?
The Arabian horse is distinct because they lived for ________ years.
questions.explanation_text: The Arabian horse lived for 51 years which is quite unusual for any normal horse.

questions.question_text: Why does Mr. Wonka collect items from the oldest things? Do you think this is the right way to begin his invention?
What happens to the volunteer who swallows four drops of the new invention? What is the name of the invention? [The Invention of Vita Wonk]
What trees does Mr. Wonka mention? Which tree does he say lives the longest?
Describe the process of the invention of Vita-Wonk.
Was the process of making Vita-Wonk easy or hard? Justify your answer using evidence from the text.
What was the effect of the drug Wonka-Vite?

What do you call these insects in your language?
How do these insects effect your health? Have a discussion in the class.
How was Vita-Wonk finally invented and what was its effect?
What are the special items in the list for? (The Invention of Vita Wonk)
Give a character sketch of Mr. Wonka.

Make a list of the trees Mr. Wonka mentions. Where do these trees grow? Try to find out from an encyclopedia. Write a short paragraph about two or three of these trees.
Name some large trees commonly found in your area. Find out something about them (How old are they? Who planted them? Do birds eat their fruit?), and write two or three sentences about each one of them.
How many of the oldest living things can you remember from Mr. Wonka's list? (Don't look back at the story!) Do you think all these things really exist, or are some of them purely imaginary?

Fill in the blanks in the recipe given below with words from the box.


shred, cooker, times, tomatoes, half, onion, oil


Easy Palak–Dal
INGREDIENTS
One ________
One cup dal
Two thin green chillies
________a teaspoon red chilli powder
Eight small bunches of palak
Two
Salt to taste
Wash and cut the vegetables; ________ the palak. Put everything in a pressure ________. Let the cooker whistle three ________, then switch it off. Fry a few cumin seeds in ________ and add to the palak–dal.

Find out something interesting about age, or growing old, and write a paragraph about it. Following are a few topics, suggested as examples:
The age profile of a country's population — does it have more young people, than old people or vice versa? What are the consequences of this?
How can we tell how old a tree, a horse, or rock is?
What is the 'life expectancy' of various living things, and various populations (how long can they reasonably expect to live)?
Did Vita-Wonk work? Justify using evidence from the text.

A family tree is a diagram that shows the relationship between the different members of a family. Fill in the family tree below with names, ages, and other details you think are relevant (you may even stick photographs if you have them). Put your family trees up in the class.
What does the lesson tell you about Vita-Wonk?

Read the following passage carefully and answer the questions that follow each of them: (1x5=5)
"What did happen?" Charlie asked. "It was fantastic!" cried Mr. Wonka. "The moment he swallowed it, he began wrinkling and shriveling up all over and his hair started dropping off and his teeth started falling out and, before I knew it, he had suddenly become an old fellow of seventy-five! And thus, my dear Charlie, was Vita-Wonk invented!"
Who was Charlie?
What did Charlie inquire about?
Who grew very old after drinking a few drops of Wonka's wonder drug?
What happened to him?
Find the word from the above passage that means the same as to wonderful.

Read the following passage carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Mr. Wonka said, "So once again I rolled up my sleeves and set to work. Once again I squeezed my brain, searching for the new recipe... I had to create age ... to make people old ... old, older, oldest ... 'Ha-ha!' I cried, for now, the ideas were beginning to come. 'What is the oldest living thing in the world? What lives longer than anything else?"
What does the phrase 'set to work' mean?
What kind of 'recipe' is mentioned here?
Why does he cry, "Ha-ha?"
Name the lesson and the author.

Read the extract given below and answer the following questions:
So once again I rolled up my sleeves and set to work. Once again I squeezed my brain, searching for the new recipe.....I had to create age... to make people old....old...older, oldest...Ha-ha!' I cried, for now, the ideas were beginning to come. What is the oldest living thing in the world? What lives longer than anything else?
Name the speaker in the above lines.
Who is he talking to?
Why did he need to work?
What is the oldest living thing in the world?

Read the following passages carefully and answer the questions that follow each of them: (1x5=5)
Mr. Wonka said, "So once again I rolled up my sleeves and set to work. Once again I squeezed my brain, searching for the new recipe... I had to create age ... to make people old ... old, older, oldest ... 'Ha-ha! I cried, for now the ideas were beginning to come. What is the oldest living thing in the world? What lives longer than anything else?"
Who was Mr. Wonka?
What did he want?
What was his first step to make a new tonic?
What does the phrase 'rolled of my sleeve' mean?
Find the word from the above passage that means opposite to destroy.

Read the following passage carefully and answer the questions that follow:
But there's no time to tell you about them all now, Charlie. Let me just say quickly that in the end, after lots of boiling and bubbling and mixing and testing in my Inventing Room, I produced one tiny cupful of oily black liquid and gave four drops of it to a brave twenty-year-old Oompa Loompa volunteer to see what happened.
What things were boiled, mixed and tested?
What was the name of the volunteer?
What did he volunteer for?
Could you have dared to volunteer for the same?

Read the following passages carefully and answer the questions that follow each of them: (1x5=5)
"All over the world, Charlie", Mr. Wonka went on "I tracked down very old and ancient animals took an important little bit of something from each one of them-a hair or an eyebrow or sometimes it was no more than an ounce or two of the jam scraped from between its toes while it was sleeping. I tracked down THE WHISTLE-PIG, THE BOBOLINK, THE SKROCK, THE POLLYFROG, THE GIANT CURLICUE, THE STINGING SLUG AND THE VENOMOUS SQUERKLE who can spit poison right into your eye from fifty yards away. But there's no time to tell you about them all now, Charlie. Let me just say quickly that in the end, after lots of boiling and bubbling and mixing and testing in my Inventing Room, I produced one tiny cupful of oily black liquid and gave four drops of it to a brave twenty-year-old Oompa-Loompa volunteer to see what happened."
Name the lesson and its author.
Why did Wonka go after very old animals and things?
How did he produce his magic liquid?
Who did Wonka select to taste the liquid that he made?
Find the word from the above passage that means the same as to bold.

Read the passage and answer the following questions:
So once again I rolled up my sleeves and set to work, once again I squeezed my brain, searching for the new recipe.........I had to create age.......... to make people old.......... old........... older, oldest............ "Haha"! I cried, for now, the idea was beginning to come. "What is the oldest living thing in the world? What lives longer than anything else?"
Name the lesson and its author.
Name the speaker and the person he is speaking to.
What is the new recipe he is searching for and what is the purpose of it?
Find the antonym of 'youngest' from the passage.
